The ELFNOTE namespace is literally "Xen" and the "domain builder" is the Xen component. (PE set confirms protected mode; PG bit 31 is among the cleared writeable bits, so paging is implicitly off; "cr4: all bits are cleared"); "ebx: contains the physical memory address where the loader has placed the boot start info structure." fact: Boot protocols disagree on the handoff register AND CPU mode for the same silicon: Linux/x86 = %rsi -> boot_params, 64-bit mode, paging ON; Multiboot2 = EAX=0x36d76289 + EBX -> info, 32-bit, paging OFF; PVH = %ebx -> start_info; arm64 = x0 -> DTB, MMU off. A free contract chosen by the loader, never dictated by the CPU.
source: kernel.org arch/x86/boot.html §1.14; GNU multiboot2 §3.3 I386 machine state; xenbits PVH; kernel.org arch/arm64/booting.html (all fetched)
evidence:
All four register/mode/paging classifications and all governance attributions verified against directly-fetched authoritative sources. No misattribution.

(1) Linux/x86 = %rsi -> boot_params, 64-bit, paging ON. kernel.org boot.html §1.14 "64-bit Boot Protocol" (title "The Linux/x86 Boot Protocol"): "At entry, the CPU must be in 64-bit mode with paging enabled... %rsi must hold the base address of the struct boot_params." Correctly attributed to Linux/kernel.org — a Linux-owned handoff contract (category: Linux legacy/free contract).

(2) Multiboot2 = EAX=0x36d76289 + EBX -> info, 32-bit, paging OFF. GNU multiboot2 §3.3 "I386 machine state" (title "Multiboot2 Specification version 2.0", gnu.org/software/grub): "EAX Must contain the magic value '0x36d76289'... EBX Must contain the 32-bit physical address of the Multiboot2 information structure... CR0 Bit 31 (PG) must be cleared. Bit 0 (PE) must be set." Header: "When the boot loader invokes the 32-bit operating system." Correctly attributed to GNU — an OS-neutral, non-Linux boot protocol (category: open standard owned by no single OS), proving boot protocols are choosable.

(3) PVH = %ebx -> start_info. xenbits "x86/HVM direct boot ABI": "ebx: contains the physical memory address where the loader has placed the boot start info structure... The format of the boot start info structure (pointed to by %ebx)." Correctly attributed to Xen Project (xenbits.xen.org). (Also cr0 PE set / cr4 cleared = 32-bit protected mode, paging off — consistent, no false paging claim made for PVH.)

(4) arm64 = x0 -> DTB, MMU off. kernel.org arm64 booting.html §4 "Call the kernel image" (title "Booting AArch64 Linux", author Will Deacon/ARM): "x0 = physical address of device tree blob (dtb) in system RAM" and "The MMU must be off." Correctly attributed to Linux/kernel.org — this is the Linux/arm64 handoff contract, NOT an ARM-silicon mandate (the ARM CPU can also hand off with MMU on, e.g. the EFI stub; requiring MMU-off is the protocol's choice).

Crux classification "A free contract chosen by the loader, never dictated by the CPU" is sound: the handoff register and the CPU mode/paging state at entry differ across protocols on the same silicon precisely because each is a software convention chosen by the protocol author — the silicon supplies all modes; which one is in effect at the jump is the loader/protocol's free choice. No defining-authority misattribution: x86 and arm64 -> Linux (kernel.org), Multiboot2 -> GNU, PVH -> Xen.

Every register/mode attribution and the silicon-vs-convention classification are exactly right. All quotes are from directly-fetched authoritative sources.

(1) Linux/x86 = %rsi -> boot_params, 64-bit, paging ON. kernel.org arch/x86/boot.html §1.14 (64-bit Boot Protocol): "At entry, the CPU must be in 64-bit mode with paging enabled... %rsi must hold the base address of the struct boot_params." CONFIRMED.

(2) Multiboot2 = EAX=0x36d76289 + EBX -> info, 32-bit, paging OFF. GNU multiboot2 §3.3 I386 machine state: "When the boot loader invokes the 32-bit operating system... EAX — Must contain the magic value '0x36d76289'... EBX — Must contain the 32-bit physical address of the Multiboot2 information structure... CR0 — Bit 31 (PG) must be cleared. Bit 0 (PE) must be set." (PG cleared = paging OFF, PE set = 32-bit protected mode.) CONFIRMED.

(3) PVH = %ebx -> start_info. xenbits.xen.org/docs/unstable/misc/pvh.html: "ebx: contains the physical memory address where the loader has placed the boot start info structure... The format of the boot start info structure (pointed to by %ebx) can be found in xen/include/public/arch-x86/hvm/start_info.h." (Also "cr0: bit 0 (PE) must be set. All the other writeable bits are cleared" = 32-bit, paging off — consistent, not overclaimed.) CONFIRMED.

(4) arm64 = x0 -> DTB, MMU off. kernel.org arch/arm64/booting.html §4: "x0 = physical address of device tree blob (dtb) in system RAM" and "Caches, MMUs — The MMU must be off." CONFIRMED.

(5) Classification "A free contract chosen by the loader, never dictated by the CPU" — CORRECT, no overclaim. These docs describe a bootloader->kernel HANDOFF CONTRACT, not internal Linux code (arm64 doc: "we will use the term boot loader simply to define all software that executes on the CPU(s) before control is passed to the Linux kernel"). The embedded proof is sound: the SAME x86 silicon is handed off three incompatible ways — Linux/x86 (rsi, 64-bit, paging on), Multiboot2 (eax+ebx, 32-bit, paging off), PVH (ebx, 32-bit, paging off). If the register or CPU mode were silicon-mandated they could not differ on identical hardware; the divergence is itself the demonstration that these are software conventions (category c/d: a protocol the loader chooses and a non-Linux OS may reject), not silicon mandates (category a). The lens scope is correctly bounded to the OS-entry handoff: silicon does dictate the CPU RESET state (x86 powers up in 16-bit real mode at the reset vector; arm64 at a reset exception level), but the FACT speaks only to the protocol handoff after firmware/loader has already set machine state, so "never dictated by the CPU" is accurately scoped and not an overclaim.

fact: aarch64 silicon entry preconditions are ARM-defined and OS-agnostic: 'The MMU must be off', 'All forms of interrupts must be masked in PSTATE.DAIF', caches cleaned to PoC, enter at EL1 or EL2. These are hardware requirements TABOS must satisfy before any Rust runs - not Linux policy.

The text itself is accurate. The GOVERNANCE/ORIGIN attribution is not. The same document is titled "Booting AArch64 Linux — The Linux Kernel documentation," states "This document... is relevant to all public releases of the AArch64 Linux kernel," and frames the list as "Before jumping into the kernel, the following conditions must be met." That is the Linux kernel's AArch64 boot PROTOCOL — a bootloader-to-kernel handoff contract owned by kernel.org (authored by Will Deacon, "based on the ARM booting document by Russell King") — i.e. exactly the "Linux policy" the FACT denies. The non-OS-agnostic, Linux-specific nature is provable from the requirement "The CPU must be in non-secure state, either in EL2... or in EL1": ARM silicon resets into the highest implemented Exception Level (EL3 when present), so "non-secure EL1/EL2" is a Linux demand, not a silicon precondition.

The four quoted requirements are verbatim-accurate in the fetched source (docs.kernel.org/arch/arm64/booting.html and the identical www.kernel.org mirror): under "CPU mode" — "All forms of interrupts must be masked in PSTATE.DAIF (Debug, SError, IRQ and FIQ)." and "The CPU must be in non-secure state, either in EL2 (RECOMMENDED in order to have access to the virtualisation extensions), or in EL1."; under "Caches, MMUs" — "The MMU must be off." and "The address range corresponding to the loaded kernel image must be cleaned to the PoC." So the requirement text is real.

However the SILICON-VS-CONVENTION classification is mis-attributed. The document self-identifies as a Linux boot protocol / handoff contract, NOT an ARM silicon mandate and NOT OS-agnostic. Title: "Booting AArch64 Linux — The Linux Kernel documentation". Scope line: "This document is based on the ARM booting document by Russell King and is relevant to all public releases of the AArch64 Linux kernel." Definition line: "we will use the term boot loader simply to define all software that executes on the CPU(s) before control is passed to the Linux kernel." And the preconditions are explicitly gated by: "Before jumping into the kernel, the following conditions must be met". These are conditions a bootloader must establish to hand off to the LINUX kernel — category (c)/(d) (a contract owned by Linux, which another OS may redefine), not category (a) (CPU-silicon-dictated).
corrections: The cited source is misclassified. arch/arm64/booting.html is the Linux kernel's arm64 boot contract (classification: (c) Linux-owned handoff/boot protocol that TABOS can reject and replace), NOT (a) "dictated by the CPU silicon," and NOT "ARM-defined and OS-agnostic." Defining authority of THIS document = Linux/kernel.org, not ARM. The FACT's claim that "these are hardware requirements... not Linux policy" inverts the actual governance: as worded and cited, they ARE Linux policy. Note the real silicon-dictated facts do exist but belong to a different authority and source: the ARM Architecture Reference Manual defines the cold-reset state (SCTLR_ELx.M reset = MMU disabled; PSTATE.DAIF masked at reset; reset taken to the highest implemented EL). To make an "ARM-defined silicon precondition" claim, cite the ARM ARM, not the Linux boot doc. Also, "enter at EL1 or EL2 (non-secure)" cannot be attributed to ARM at all — it is purely the Linux boot protocol's requirement.

Do not classify these as "ARM-defined and OS-agnostic ... hardware requirements ... not Linux policy." The cited source is precisely Linux policy: the AArch64 Linux boot protocol, a bootloader-to-kernel handoff contract published as Linux Kernel Documentation (authored by Will Deacon, will.deacon@arm.com, but a kernel.org doc, not an ARM Architecture Reference Manual clause). They are NOT silicon-mandated: the CPU does not force an OS to be entered with MMU off, DAIF masked, caches cleaned to PoC, and at non-secure EL1/EL2. fact: struct boot_params (the x86_64 'zero-page') is at most 4096 bytes, and its setup_header is currently 119 bytes long, permanently capped at 144 bytes. In the 64-bit boot protocol the kernel is entered at (loaded-kernel-start + 0x200) with the CPU ALREADY in 64-bit mode with paging enabled, a 4G-flat GDT (__BOOT_CS=0x10, __BOOT_DS=0x18), interrupts disabled, and %rsi holding the boot_params base. Implementing this is 'filling a struct', not running Linux code.
evidence:
All claims verified against the authoritative source https://www.kernel.org/doc/html/latest/arch/x86/boot.html (title: "The Linux/x86 Boot Protocol"), fetched directly.

SIZES (§1.4 kernel_info): The source states verbatim: "setup_header is permanently limited to 144 bytes due to the reach of the 2-byte jump field, which doubles as a length field for the structure, combined with the size of the 'hole' in struct boot_params that a protected-mode loader or the BIOS stub has to copy it into. It is currently 119 bytes long, which leaves us with 25 very precious bytes. This isn't something that can be fixed without revising the boot protocol entirely, breaking backwards compatibility." And: "boot_params proper is limited to 4096 bytes, but can be arbitrarily extended by adding setup_data entries." => Confirms 4096-byte cap, setup_header currently 119 bytes, permanently capped at 144 bytes (all exact).

64-BIT BOOT PROTOCOL (§1.14): Verbatim: "the kernel is started by jumping to the 64-bit kernel entry point, which is the start address of loaded 64-bit kernel plus 0x200." / "At entry, the CPU must be in 64-bit mode with paging enabled." / "a GDT must be loaded with the descriptors for selectors __BOOT_CS(0x10) and __BOOT_DS(0x18); both descriptors must be 4G flat segment; __BOOT_CS must have execute/read permission, and __BOOT_DS must have read/write permission; CS must be __BOOT_CS and DS, ES, SS must be __BOOT_DS; interrupt must be disabled; %rsi must hold the base address of the struct boot_params." => Confirms entry at start+0x200, 64-bit mode + paging enabled, 4G-flat GDT, __BOOT_CS=0x10, __BOOT_DS=0x18, interrupts disabled, %rsi=boot_params base. Every element exact.

GOVERNANCE/ORIGIN LENS: Defining authority is correctly stated as Linux/kernel.org (document is literally titled "The Linux/x86 Boot Protocol"). No misattribution to OASIS, Intel, ARM, or SysV. The classification "filling a struct, not running Linux code" is the correct (c)/handoff-contract characterization: per the spec the bootloader's obligation is to set up struct boot_params and jump to the entry point ("the boot loader should also fill the additional fields of the struct boot_params"); it does not execute Linux kernel code. It is a Linux-owned handoff ABI that any non-Linux bootloader (e.g. Firecracker, rust-vmm) can implement without running Linux. Correctly attributed and correctly classified.

And: "boot_params proper is limited to 4096 bytes, but can be arbitrarily extended by adding setup_data entries." => 119 bytes current, 144-byte permanent cap, 4096-byte boot_params cap all CONFIRMED verbatim (119+25=144 checks out; the 4096 applies to the struct proper, with setup_data linked entries being separate, exactly matching the FACT's "at most 4096 bytes").

Adversarial check passes: the FACT does NOT misattribute any entry requirement (64-bit mode, paging, GDT, IF-disabled, %rsi) to the CPU silicon — it presents them strictly as the protocol's chosen entry state, which is exactly right. fact: PVH ('x86/HVM direct boot ABI', a Xen-originated but VMM-portable ABI) is signaled by the XEN_ELFNOTE_PHYS32_ENTRY ELF note; the domain builder enters at that note's address with EBX = physical address of the hvm_start_info structure, CR0.PE set, all other writable CR0 bits and all of CR4 cleared (32-bit, paging off). The start_info carries rsdp_paddr for ACPI. No zero-page, no real-mode legacy.
evidence:
Every claim is directly supported by the authoritative Xen source (https://xenbits.xen.org/docs/unstable/misc/pvh.html), and the governance/origin attribution (Xen-originated boot ABI, not Linux/Intel/ARM/OASIS) is correct.

Title: "x86/HVM direct boot ABI". Origin: "Since the Xen entry point into the kernel can be different from the native entry point, a ELFNOTE is used..." with `ELFNOTE(Xen, XEN_ELFNOTE_PHYS32_ENTRY, .long, xen_start32)`.

Signaling note: "The presence of the XEN_ELFNOTE_PHYS32_ENTRY note indicates that the kernel supports the boot ABI described in this document."

Entry register/state (verbatim): "The domain builder must load the kernel into the guest memory space and jump into the entry point defined at XEN_ELFNOTE_PHYS32_ENTRY" with — "ebx: contains the physical memory address where the loader has placed the boot start info structure." / "cr0: bit 0 (PE) must be set. All the other writeable bits are cleared." / "cr4: all bits are cleared." / "cs: must be a 32-bit read/execute code segment with a base of '0' and a limit of '0xFFFFFFFF'." / "eflags: bit 17 (VM) must be cleared." The start-info struct is in xen/include/public/arch-x86/hvm/start_info.h (hvm_start_info).

ACPI: "A pointer to the position of the RSDP in memory (if present) can be fetched from the start info structure that's passed at boot time (field rsdp_paddr)."

These confirm: PVH/x86-HVM-direct-boot ABI, XEN_ELFNOTE_PHYS32_ENTRY signaling, EBX=phys addr of hvm_start_info, CR0.PE set + other writable CR0 bits and all CR4 cleared (CR0.PG cleared => paging off; CS 32-bit), rsdp_paddr in start_info, and 32-bit protected-mode entry (VM bit cleared => no real-mode legacy) with boot data via start_info rather than a Linux zero-page. Origin correctly attributed to Xen; it is a non-Linux, choosable boot protocol — no misattribution to OASIS/Intel/ARM/SysV/Linux.

All core technical claims are directly quoted-confirmed by the OASIS-independent Xen source (xenbits.xen.org/docs/unstable/misc/pvh.html, §1 "x86/HVM direct boot ABI"):

1. ELF-note signaling: "The presence of the XEN_ELFNOTE_PHYS32_ENTRY note indicates that the kernel supports the boot ABI described in this document."

2. Entry + EBX: "The domain builder must load the kernel into the guest memory space and jump into the entry point defined at XEN_ELFNOTE_PHYS32_ENTRY" and "ebx: contains the physical memory address where the loader has placed the boot start info structure." (struct type hvm_start_info per start_info.h).

3. CR0/CR4 state: "cr0: bit 0 (PE) must be set. All the other writeable bits are cleared. cr4: all bits are cleared." -> paging off (PG is a cleared writable bit), 32-bit (CS is a 32-bit r/x code segment, base 0, limit 0xFFFFFFFF).

4. ACPI handoff: "A pointer to the position of the RSDP in memory (if present) can be fetched from the start info structure that's passed at boot time (field rsdp_paddr)."

5. No zero-page / no real-mode: PE set = protected mode; "eflags: bit 17 (VM) must be cleared" = no virtual-8086/real-mode; boot data via hvm_start_info, with no Linux zero-page referenced anywhere in the doc.

The mandated entry state (CR0.PE=1, paging off, CR4=0) is a deliberate ABI convention that DIVERGES from the x86 silicon reset state (real mode, PE=0, the architecturally-mandated power-on state) -- which actually reinforces that boot handoff is convention, not silicon. Verified against the fetched Limine PROTOCOL.md (raw.githubusercontent.com/limine-bootloader/limine-protocol/trunk/PROTOCOL.md). The repo/ownership attribution and every machine-state claim check out, and — through the silicon-vs-convention lens — nothing silicon-mandated is mislabeled as avoidable, nor is any free choice mislabeled as silicon.

QUOTED SUPPORT (section "## Machine State at Entry" -> "### x86-64"):
- 64-bit long mode + paging enabled: "PE is enabled (`cr0`), ET is enabled (`cr0`), WP is enabled (`cr0`), PG is enabled (`cr0`), PAE is enabled (`cr4`), LME and LMA are enabled (`EFER`). NX is enabled (`EFER`) (if it is available)." (LMA = Long Mode Active -> handoff is in 64-bit long mode with paging on.) Matches "PG/PAE/LME/LMA/NX enabled."
- Selectors: "At entry, `CS` is loaded with `0x28` and `DS`, `ES`, `SS`, `FS`, `GS` are loaded with `0x30`, pointing to the 64-bit code and data descriptors respectively." Matches CS=0x28; DS/ES/SS/FS/GS=0x30.
- Bootloader-provided GDT with full descriptor set: "The GDT register is loaded to point to a GDT, in bootloader-reclaimable memory, with at least the following entries... Null descriptor / 16-bit code / 16-bit data / 32-bit code / 32-bit data / 64-bit code / 64-bit data descriptor." Matches "bootloader-provided GDT (full 16/32/64-bit descriptor set)."
- Higher-Half Direct Map: the spec defines an "### HHDM (Higher Half Direct Map) Feature" (LIMINE_HHDM_REQUEST_ID) and the executable is loaded "at or above `0xffffffff80000000`"; "HHDM (Higher Half Direct Map) covers all memory map regions." Confirms the HHDM claim.
- Data-passing via in-executable requests/responses (not a single struct pointer): "## Features ... The protocol is centered around the concept of request/response ... In C terms, a feature is comprised of 2 structures: the request, and the response." Request id is "an 8-byte aligned magic number that the bootloader will scan for inside the loaded executable image to find requests ... Requests may be located anywhere inside the loaded executable image." Plus "## Requests Delimiters" start/end markers. This is fundamentally different from a single info-struct pointer handed in a register (e.g. multiboot2's EBX) — confirming the contrast claimed.

SILICON-VS-CONVENTION JUDGEMENT (the crux): The classification is exactly right. The data-passing mechanism (magic-tagged request/response structs embedded in the executable image, scanned by the bootloader) is pure convention owned by the Limine community — category (d), freely chosen/owned. The handoff operating mode (64-bit long, paging on, supervisor) is also a free protocol choice: x86-64 silicon supports real / 32-bit protected / 64-bit long modes, and other vendor-neutral protocols hand off differently (multiboot2 and PVH hand off in 32-bit protected mode). The specific selector values (0x28/0x30), the GDT layout, and the HHDM are Limine conventions, not CPU requirements. So the FACT's conclusion — "even the privilege mode and data-passing mechanism at handoff are freely chosen" — is correctly supported and does NOT overclaim avoidability of anything silicon-mandated.

Critically, the FACT also does not commit the inverse error (calling silicon "convention"): it lists PG/PAE/LME/LMA as descriptive machine state but only attributes the *mode choice* and *data mechanism* to free choice. That is precise: per Intel SDM Vol.3 / AMD APM, CR0.PG, CR4.PAE, EFER.LME (and CPU-set EFER.LMA) are the silicon-mandated prerequisites that a 64-bit-long-mode choice forces — they are the implementation of the chosen mode, not independently free toggles. The FACT does not claim those bits are individually optional, so there is no overclaim. NX is correctly hedged by the spec itself as "if it is available" (a silicon-capability gate), and the FACT does not claim NX is unconditional.
corrections: No correction to the classification or attribution. One minor wording nuance (does not affect the verdict): the FACT calls it an "in-executable 'requests/responses' section." In the current base revisions (1+), the bootloader scans the WHOLE loaded executable image for magic-tagged requests bounded by start/end markers, not a dedicated named ELF section. The named `.limine_reqs` section was the base-revision-0 mechanism, now explicitly deprecated: "This behaviour is deprecated and removed as of base revision 1... if the executable file contains a `.limine_reqs` executable section, the bootloader will, instead of scanning the whole executable's loaded image for requests, fetch the requests from a NULL-terminated array of pointers..." The substance — requests/responses embedded in the executable rather than a single struct pointer — is fully correct.

No correction to the classification or attribution — confirmed. Two non-material precision notes for the record (neither refutes the FACT): (1) The literal spec heading is "## Machine State at Entry" with subheading "### x86-64"; the FACT's parenthetical citation "### x86-64 entry machine state" is a paraphrase but points to the correct section. (2) In current base revisions (1+) requests are not a single named ELF section but magic-tagged structs scanned anywhere in the loaded image (the named `.limine_reqs` *section* is explicitly "deprecated and removed as of base revision 1"); the FACT's phrase "in-executable 'requests/responses' section" remains an accurate characterization of the mechanism and of the contrast with a single struct pointer.
